title: End-to-End Pipeline for Shift-Share (Bartik) Instrumental Variables
text: design hand, credibility check finished single call.       returns small object readable print() method; checks run automatically inside ssbartik() / ssb_pipeline() pass corresponding arguments (pre_y, placebo_y, shock_covariates, covariates).
code: d   <- ssb_design(df$data, df$shares, df$shocks,                   controls = "w1", weights = "pop", exogenous = "share") rot <- ssb_rotemberg(d)                    # Rotemberg-weight decomposition plot(rot, n = 6)                           # see below est <- ssb_estimate(d)                     # EHW / cluster / AKM / AKM0 (add iid/two-way on request) ssb_plot_ci(est) # instrument strength & internal consistency ssb_first_stage(d)               # standard robust F + exposure-robust "effective" F ssb_equivalence(d)               # location-level SSIV == shock-level IV (sanity check) ssb_overid(d)                    # do the per-share estimates agree? (Cochran's Q) # identifying-assumption checks ssb_shock_balance(d, shock_covariates = sc)  # shocks vs. pre-determined characteristics ssb_pretrend(d, pre_y = "y_pre")             # does exposure predict pre-trends? ssb_placebo(d, placebo_y = "y_plac")         # full IV on an outcome that shouldn't move ssb_ri(d, R = 999, block = "grp")            # AR-style randomization inference (shocks permuted within blocks) # sensitivity to influential shocks ws<- ssb_weight_summary(d, covariates = "w1")     # who carries the weight? (Î± vs. Î˛â‚–, F, covariates) plot(ws) ssb_drop_top(d, n = 5)                        # re-estimate without the top-weight shocks ssb_recenter(d, method = "permute", block = "grp")     # block-wise recentering (within-block mean shock)

title: ssBartik: End-to-End Pipeline for Shift-Share (Bartik) IV Designs â€” ssBartik-package
text: Shift-share / Bartik instrumental-variable analysis R spread across several single-purpose packages: ShiftShareSE (Kolesar) AKM inference, bartik.weight (jjchern) Rotemberg weights, ssaggregate (Butts) shock-level aggregation. ssBartik brings construction, diagnostics, estimation, exposure-robust inference publication-ready visualisation one consistent workflow, organised around two identification routes modern literature (exogenous shares vs. exogenous shifts).

Core methods: Adao, Kolesar & Morales (2019, QJE); Borusyak, Hull & Jaravel (2022, REStud), Borusyak, Hull & Jaravel (2025, "Practical Guide Shift-Share Instruments") Borusyak, Hull & Jaravel (2025, "Design-based identification formula instruments: review", Econometrics Journal 28(1), 83-108); Goldsmith-Pinkham, Sorkin & Swift (2020, AER).

what: Aggregate a shift-share design to the shock (shifter) level â€” ssb_aggregate
title: Aggregate a shift-share design to the shock (shifter) level â€” ssb_aggregate
text: Collapses unit-level design one row per shock, following Borusyak-Hull-Jaravel (2022) equivalence. controls partialled outcome treatment (weighted FWL), shock \(n\) gets exposure weight \(s_n=\sum_i e_i s_{}\) exposure-weighted means \(\bar y_n=\sum_i e_i s_{}\tilde y_i/s_n\) \(\bar x_n\) similarly. Running IV \(\bar y_n\) \(\bar x_n\) instrument \(g_n\) weights \(s_n\) reproduces location-level shift-share estimate exactly (see [ssb_equivalence()]).

title: Define a shift-share (Bartik) IV design â€” ssb_design
text: data unit-level `data.frame`: one row per location (location-period). Must contain `y`, `x`, `location`, `controls`, `weights`, `cluster` columns referenced . shares long `data.frame` exposure shares columns `location`, `sector`, share column (`share_col`), `time` panels. shocks `data.frame` shocks columns `sector`, shock column (`shock_col`), `time` panels. y, x Column names (strings) outcome endogenous treatment. location, sector Column names unit sector identifiers. time Optional column name period identifier (present `data`, `shares` `shocks`) panel designs. controls Optional character vector control columns `data`. Numeric columns enter linearly; factor character columns expanded dummies, period region fixed effects can supplied factors (panel shift-share designs, period fixed effects usually essential â€” shocks compared within periods). weights Optional column name regression weights `data`. cluster Optional column name clustering variable `data`. share_col Name exposure-share column `shares` (default `"share"`). shock_col Name shock (shift) column `shocks` (default `"shock"`). exogenous identification route emphasise downstream: `"shift"` (shocks) `"share"` (shares). `"shock"`/`"shares"` accepted aliases.

title: Define a shift-share (Bartik) IV design â€” ssb_design
text: object class `ssb_design`.

title: Define a shift-share (Bartik) IV design â€” ssb_design
text: **instrument constructed identically** whichever identification route take; `exogenous` argument governs *diagnostics* *controls* appropriate downstream (see [ssb_pipeline()]). Set `exogenous = "share"` exogenous-shares route (Goldsmith-Pinkham, Sorkin Swift 2020; Rotemberg-weight diagnostics) `exogenous = "shift"` exogenous-shocks route (Borusyak, Hull Jaravel 2022; Adao, Kolesar Morales 2019; shock-level diagnostics AKM inference).

what: Overidentification / cross-instrument homogeneity test â€” ssb_overid
title: Overidentification / cross-instrument homogeneity test â€” ssb_overid
text: Treats sector's share separate instrument tests whether just-identified estimates \(\hat\beta_n\) mutually consistent, using precision-weighted Cochran's Q statistic \(Q=\sum_n (\hat\beta_n-\bar\beta)^2/\widehat{\mathrm{Var}}(\hat\beta_n)\) referred \(\chi^2_{K-1}\) distribution. Rejection points failure shares/shocks exogeneity **** treatment-effect heterogeneity across instruments (Goldsmith-Pinkham, Sorkin & Swift 2020). weak instruments -weighted automatically; use `min_F` drop near-dead instruments entirely.

title: Overidentification / cross-instrument homogeneity test â€” ssb_overid
text: **Caveat.** \(\hat\beta_n\) estimated ** sample therefore mutually correlated; \(\chi^2_{K-1}\) reference treats independent ignores covariance. Read p-value heuristic screen gross cross-instrument disagreement, formal overidentification test â€” latter, use J-type test estimator robust many instruments (e.g. HFUL-based test Goldsmith-Pinkham, Sorkin & Swift 2020).

code: sim <- ssb_simulate(n_loc = 80, n_sec = 10, seed = 1) d <- ssb_design(sim$data, sim$shares, sim$shocks, exogenous = "share") ssb_overid(d) #> <ssBartik overidentification test (cross-instrument homogeneity)> #>   Q = 2.44 on 9 df,  p = 0.9824 #>   I^2 = 0.0%   precision-weighted mean beta = 1.5435 #>   instruments used: 10 (dropped 0; 9 weak, F<10) #>   small p => reject a common coefficient (exogeneity failure OR heterogeneity) #>   (heuristic: the beta_k are mutually correlated, so the chi-square reference is approximate)

what: Recenter the shocks (Borusyak & Hull) â€” ssb_recenter
title: Recenter the shocks (Borusyak & Hull) â€” ssb_recenter
text: Recentering removes expected instrument implied shock-assignment process, identification comes deviations shocks (conditional) mean. Two methods: `"demean"` (default): subtract single exposure-weighted mean shock         \(\bar g\). Leaves point estimate unchanged makes         identifying variation explicit. `"permute"`: subtract *block-specific simple average* shock, .e.         recenter within exchangeability groups. uniform within-block         permutation every cell block equally likely receive         block's shocks, \(E[g_n]\) unweighted within-block         mean; subtracting gives expectation instrument         assignment process (Borusyak & Hull), computed analytically.         `block` recenters grand unweighted mean. randomization-inference p-values based permutation idea, see [ssb_ri()].

what: Rotemberg weights for a Bartik instrument â€” ssb_rotemberg
title: Rotemberg weights for a Bartik instrument â€” ssb_rotemberg
text: Decomposes shift-share 2SLS estimate weighted sum just-identified estimates use sector's share single instrument, following Goldsmith-Pinkham, Sorkin Swift (2020): $$\hat\beta = \sum_n \hat\alpha_n \hat\beta_n,\qquad       \hat\alpha_n = \frac{g_n\, \tilde s_n' \tilde x}{\sum_{n'} g_{n'}\,       \tilde s_{n'}' \tilde x},$$ tildes denote residualisation controls (, panels, sector-cells sector \(\times\) period pairs). weights \(\hat\alpha_n\) sum one measure sensitivity \(\hat\beta\) misspecification sector's instrument; small number large weights warning sign. Unlike Goodman-Bacon weights, negative Rotemberg weights automatically problematic.

title: One-call shift-share analysis â€” ssbartik
code: # Bring your own data; this is a small synthetic design for illustration. set.seed(1) n_loc <- 60L; n_sec <- 8L shares <- expand.grid(location = seq_len(n_loc), sector = seq_len(n_sec)) shares$share <- stats::runif(nrow(shares)) tot <- tapply(shares$share, shares$location, sum) shares$share <- shares$share / tot[as.character(shares$location)] shocks <- data.frame(sector = seq_len(n_sec), shock = stats::rnorm(n_sec)) Z <- tapply(shares$share, list(shares$location, shares$sector), sum) Z[is.na(Z)] <- 0 inst <- as.numeric(Z %*% shocks$shock) dat <- data.frame(location = seq_len(n_loc),                   x = 4 * inst + stats::rnorm(n_loc, sd = 0.3)) dat$y <- 1.2 * dat$x + stats::rnorm(n_loc, sd = 0.3) res <- ssbartik(dat, shares, shocks, exogenous = "share") res #> == ssBartik result ============================================== #> route : exogenous SHARE #>  #> <ssBartik estimate> #>   first-stage F : 324.9 #>         method estimate std.error conf.low conf.high           note #>            EHW     1.23    0.0382     1.15      1.30                #>  Clustering SE     1.23        NA       NA        NA no cluster var #>            AKM     1.23    0.0268     1.17      1.28                #>           AKM0     1.23    0.3156     1.09      2.33                #>  #> <ssBartik first-stage strength> #>   standard robust F         : 324.9 #>   effective (exposure) F    : 1168.7 #>  #> <ssBartik equivalence check> #>   location-level SSIV : 1.226347 #>   shock-level IV      : 1.226347 #>   |difference|        : 0.00e+00  (match) #>  #> <ssBartik overidentification test (cross-instrument homogeneity)> #>   Q = 5.30 on 7 df,  p = 0.6235 #>   I^2 = 0.0%   precision-weighted mean beta = 1.2230 #>   instruments used: 8 (dropped 0; 4 weak, F<10) #>   small p => reject a common coefficient (exogeneity failure OR heterogeneity) #>   (heuristic: the beta_k are mutually correlated, so the chi-square reference is approximate) #>  #> <ssBartik Rotemberg weights> #>   overall beta_hat : 1.2263 #>   sum positive alpha : 1.000   sum negative alpha : 0.000 #>   largest weight   : alpha = 0.462 (6) #>   ! one shock carries |alpha| = 0.46; check robustness via ssb_drop_top() #>   top 5 sectors by |alpha|: #>  sector  alpha beta     F #>       6 0.4620 1.21 36.89 #>       5 0.1370 1.32 12.01 #>       2 0.1227 1.11 10.41 #>       1 0.1003 1.34 11.99 #>       4 0.0888 1.27  6.02 #>   (negative weights are not by themselves a red flag; see GPSS 2020) #>  #> <ssBartik Rotemberg-weight summary> #>   largest weight: alpha = 0.462 (6) #>   cor(alpha, beta_k) = -0.21   cor(alpha, F) = 0.98 #>   top shocks by |alpha|: #>  sector  alpha beta     F      g #>       6 0.4620 1.21 36.89 -2.000 #>       5 0.1370 1.32 12.01  1.163 #>       2 0.1227 1.11 10.41  1.034 #>       1 0.1003 1.34 11.99  0.707 #>       4 0.0888 1.27  6.02 -0.879 #>  #> [leave-one-out] overall beta = 1.2263  #>  sector  alpha beta_drop #>       6 0.4620      1.24 #>       5 0.1370      1.21 #>       2 0.1227      1.24 #>       1 0.1003      1.21 #>       4 0.0888      1.22 #> ================================================================= # \donttest{ autoplot(res)                       # headline Rotemberg figure  autoplot(res$estimate)              # CI comparison  # }

title: ssBartik 0.1.1
text: First release. end--end toolkit shift-share (Bartik) instrumental variables, spanning identification routes (exogenous shares exogenous shocks) instrument construction estimation, inference, credibility diagnostics. Estimation: ssb_design(), ssbartik(), ssb_estimate(), reporting panel confidence intervals (IID, EHW, exposure-robust AKM / AKM0 default; cluster two-way available request). Rotemberg / GPSS diagnostics: ssb_rotemberg(), ssb_weight_summary(), ssb_loo(), ssb_drop_top(). Shock-level (Borusyak-Hull-Jaravel) tools: ssb_aggregate(), ssb_shock_iv(), ssb_equivalence(), ssb_recenter(). Credibility checks: ssb_first_stage(), ssb_overid(), ssb_share_balance(), ssb_shock_balance(), ssb_pretrend(), ssb_placebo(), ssb_ri(). Output: autoplot() / ssb_plot_*() figures (including ssb_plot_ci() interval comparison), paste-ready tables via format(x, "latex" / "markdown") rendered plot() Rotemberg table.
